name: Pele
description: Pele is the Hawaiian goddess of volcanoes, fire, lightning, wind, and dance. A complex and powerful figure, she is revered for her creative and destructive capabilities, embodying both the life-giving warmth and devastating force of volcanic eruptions. Oral traditions depict her as a passionate and volatile deity, often portrayed as jealous and quick to anger, yet also capable of great generosity and love. Pele's presence is deeply felt in the volcanic landscapes of the Hawaiian Islands, particularly on the Big Island, where the active volcanoes of Kīlauea and Mauna Loa are considered her sacred homes. Her fiery temperament and association with lava flows serve as a constant reminder of the potent forces shaping the islands. Pele is a central figure in Hawaiian mythology, influencing not only the physical environment but also the cultural and spiritual lives of the people. She is a symbol of both creation and destruction, reminding us of the constant cycle of change and renewal inherent in the natural world. She is also associated with the hula dance, and is considered its patron deity. Offerings and prayers are made to her to appease her and ensure the safety of those who live near active volcanoes.
subtitle: Goddess of Volcanoes
titles: Ka wahine ʻai honua (The woman who devours the land)
Wahine ʻIli ʻālohilohi (Woman with shining skin)

keyMyths.title: Pele's Journey to Hawaiʻi
keyMyths.description: This myth recounts Pele's journey from Kahiki (a distant land, often associated with Tahiti) to the Hawaiian Islands. Driven from her home by her water goddess sister, Nā-maka-o-Kahaʻi, Pele traveled from island to island, creating volcanoes as she went. She finally found a permanent home on Kīlauea, where she continues to reside. This journey symbolizes the volcanic formation of the Hawaiian Islands and the ongoing battle between fire and water. The story highlights Pele’s determination and her relationship with her family.
keyMyths.source: Beckwith, Martha Warren. *Hawaiian Mythology*. University of Hawai'i Press, 1970.

keyMyths.title: Pele and Kamapuaʻa
keyMyths.description: The myth of Pele and Kamapuaʻa tells of a tumultuous love affair between the fire goddess and the pig-god Kamapuaʻa. Their relationship was marked by constant conflict due to their contrasting natures: Pele representing fire and destruction, and Kamapuaʻa representing fertility and growth. Their battles often resulted in dramatic transformations of the landscape, with lava flows meeting lush vegetation. Ultimately, they reached a compromise, dividing the island between them, thus explaining the diverse environments found in Hawaiʻi.
keyMyths.source: Kamakau, Samuel Manaiakalani. *Tales and Traditions of the People of Old*. Bishop Museum Press, 1991.

keyMyths.title: Pele and the Old Woman
keyMyths.description: This story tells of Pele disguising herself as an old woman and asking for food and shelter. Those who treated her kindly were rewarded, while those who were inhospitable faced her wrath. This myth serves as a cautionary tale about the importance of hospitality and respect for elders, reflecting the cultural values of Hawaiian society. It reinforces the idea that Pele can appear in unexpected forms and that one should always treat others with kindness, as they may be in the presence of a powerful deity.
keyMyths.source: Westervelt, William Drake. *Hawaiian Legends of Volcanoes*. Charles E. Tuttle Company, 1963.

sources.author: Beckwith, Martha Warren
sources.description: A comprehensive collection of Hawaiian myths and legends, providing detailed accounts of Pele and her role in the creation of the islands.
sources.text: A comprehensive collection of Hawaiian myths and legends, providing detailed accounts of Pele and her role in the creation of the islands.

sources.author: Kamakau, Samuel Manaiakalani
sources.work: Tales and Traditions of the People of Old
sources.description: A valuable source of Hawaiian history and culture, including stories about Pele and her interactions with other deities and humans.
sources.text: A valuable source of Hawaiian history and culture, including stories about Pele and her interactions with other deities and humans.

sources.author: Westervelt, William Drake
sources.description: Focuses specifically on the legends surrounding Pele and the volcanoes of Hawaiʻi, offering insights into her power and influence.
sources.text: Focuses specifically on the legends surrounding Pele and the volcanoes of Hawaiʻi, offering insights into her power and influence.

associations.name: Fire
associations.significance: Pele's most prominent association, representing her volatile nature and destructive power, but also warmth and creation.

associations.name: Red
associations.significance: Symbolizes fire, lava, and Pele's passionate, sometimes destructive, energy.

associations.name: Volcanic Eruption
associations.significance: Represents Pele's power, both destructive and capable of creating new land.

associations.name: Wild Boar
associations.significance: One of Pele's kinolau (physical forms). Represents strength, ferocity, and connection to the earth.

associations.name: Lightning
associations.significance: Associated with Pele's swift and powerful strikes, reflecting her quick temper and ability to inflict damage from afar.

associations.name: Creation
associations.significance: Despite her destructive nature, Pele is also responsible for creating new land through volcanic activity, symbolizing renewal and rebirth.

worshipPractices.name: Hula
worshipPractices.description: Traditional Hawaiian dance performed to honor Pele and other deities. Certain hula styles are specifically dedicated to Pele, invoking her presence and power through movement and chant.

worshipPractices.name: Offerings
worshipPractices.description: Offerings of ohelo berries, gin, flowers, and other items are left at volcanic craters and other sacred sites to appease Pele and seek her blessings. These offerings acknowledge her dominion over the land.

festivals.name: Pele Day
festivals.description: While not a formal holiday, Pele Day is celebrated by many Hawaiians through hula, storytelling, and offerings, honoring her impact on Hawaiian culture and the islands' formation.

festivals.name: Merrie Monarch Festival
festivals.description: Although not exclusively for Pele, this prestigious hula competition often features performances dedicated to her, showcasing her significance in Hawaiian dance and mythology. It is a week long celebration of Hawaiian culture.

cultural.modernLegacy: Pele remains a potent symbol of Hawaiian identity and the power of nature. She is featured in art, literature, and popular culture, and is a reminder of the ongoing volcanic activity shaping the Hawaiian Islands. Her name and image are used in many commercial contexts and tourist attractions.

extendedContent.title: Origins & Birth
extendedContent.content: Pele's origins are rooted in Polynesian mythology, with varying accounts of her birthplace and parentage. One common narrative places her birth in Tahiti (Kahiki), from where she journeyed to Hawai'i. She is often described as the daughter of Haumea, a primordial earth mother, and Kane Milohai, a creator god. Her siblings include other significant deities such as Namakaokaha'i, a sea goddess who becomes Pele's rival. The myths surrounding Pele's birth often involve conflict and exile, setting the stage for her fiery journey to establish her dominion over the Hawaiian Islands. These stories emphasize her divine lineage and her connection to both the earth and the cosmos, highlighting her role as a powerful force of nature.

extendedContent.title: Major Myths & Stories
extendedContent.content: Pele is central to numerous Hawaiian myths and legends. One of the most well-known stories involves her tumultuous relationship with her sister, Namakaokaha'i, the sea goddess. Jealousy and rivalry between the sisters led to Pele's exile and her search for a permanent home among the Hawaiian Islands. As she moved from island to island, she dug fire pits, but each was extinguished by Namakaokaha'i's waves. Eventually, Pele found refuge on Kilauea, where she established her permanent home. Another important myth tells of Pele's fiery temper and her interactions with mortals, often resulting in destruction or unexpected blessings. These stories serve as explanations for volcanic activity and natural phenomena, while also providing moral lessons about respecting the power of nature and the consequences of unchecked emotions.

extendedContent.title: Worship & Cult
extendedContent.content: The worship of Pele has been a significant aspect of Hawaiian culture for centuries. Traditional practices involve offering gifts to Pele at volcanic craters and other sacred sites. These offerings, which include ohelo berries, flowers, and other symbolic items, are meant to appease the goddess and seek her favor. Hula dances, particularly those performed in the style of 'Aiha'a, are also dedicated to Pele, invoking her presence and power through rhythmic movements and chants. Priests and priestesses played a crucial role in mediating between the people and Pele, interpreting her signs and conducting rituals. Although formal temples dedicated solely to Pele were rare, her presence was felt in all aspects of Hawaiian life, and respect for her power was deeply ingrained in the culture. Today, many continue to honor Pele through offerings and reverence, recognizing her enduring influence on the Hawaiian Islands.

extendedContent.title: Legacy & Influence
extendedContent.content: Pele's legacy extends far beyond ancient mythology, continuing to shape Hawaiian culture and identity in modern times. She remains a powerful symbol of the islands' volcanic origins and the dynamic forces that continue to shape the landscape. Pele's image and name are widely used in art, literature, and popular culture, serving as a reminder of the connection between the Hawaiian people and their environment. The ongoing volcanic activity on the Big Island of Hawai'i is often attributed to Pele's presence, reinforcing her status as a living deity. Furthermore, Pele's stories and legends continue to inspire artists, writers, and dancers, ensuring that her influence will endure for generations to come. She represents not only the destructive power of nature but also the creative potential of volcanic activity, symbolizing renewal and rebirth.

shortDescription: Pele is the fiery Hawaiian goddess of volcanoes, fire, lightning, wind, and dance, revered in Polynesian mythology for both her creative and destructive powers.
